variables.content: Because direct thermodynamic-limit extrapolation of the discrete-angle DMC-determined Fermi liquid parameters proved unreliable, a set of “best available” numerical recommendations for the Fermi liquid parameters was assembled by combining independently published QMC results for related quantities: quasiparticle effective masses $m^{*}$ obtained from DMC energy-band calculations and smooth QMC-based parameterizations of the ground-state correlation energy.  Using the literature effective masses together with compressibility and spin-susceptibility values derived from the total-energy parameterizations and the Landau relations yields recommended values such as, for the paramagnetic 2D HEG: at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}^{s}=-0.495(2)$, $F_{0}^{a}=-0.346(2)$, $F_{1}^{s}=-0.053(3)$; at $r_{s}=5$ and $10$ analogous recommended values are provided; and for the ferromagnetic 2D HEG recommended values include at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}=-0.428(2)$ and $\bar{F}_1=-0.159(3)$.  These assembled values combine ground-state energy parameterizations and published effective masses to provide pragmatic numerical recommendations when direct thermodynamic-limit determinations of $F_l$ from discrete-angle DMC data are unreliable.

The quasiparticle effective masses m^{*} reported in the literature from DMC fits to the single-particle energy band and the ground-state total-energy parametrizations reported in the literature as continuous functions E_{\mathrm{corr}}(r_s,\zeta) of density parameter r_s and spin polarization \zeta are mutually consistent within their stated uncertainties and can be combined algebraically via standard Fermi-liquid thermodynamic relations (for example F_1^{s} = m^{*}-1 for the paramagnetic HEG and evaluation of F_0^{s,a} from derivatives of E(r_s,\zeta)) to produce numerical estimates of the dimensionless Fermi liquid parameters F_l for the 2D HEG.
      Refs. 11,23,24

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

The homogeneous electron gas (HEG) model represents the valence electrons of a crystalline metal by a translation-invariant free-electron Fermi sea characterized by a single Fermi wavevector $k_{F}$ and Fermi energy $\\varepsilon_{F}$ determined from the valence electron density; for order-of-magnitude estimates of the ANUBIS anomalous-response amplitudes and qualitative $q$ dependence for elemental metals, electronic-structure details beyond the HEG (band anisotropy, multiple Fermi sheets, interband transitions, and realistic effective masses) are taken to be negligible.

For the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG), the leading finite-size error in Fermi-liquid-derived quantities such as the angular-momentum Fermi liquid parameters F_l or the Fourier-projected quantities F_l/m^{*} behaves asymptotically as a power law in the finite-system particle number N with exponent -1/4; equivalently,
      \Delta F_l(N) \equiv F_l(N)-F_l(\infty) \propto N^{-1/4},
      so that fitting computed F_l(N) values to the form c + a N^{-1/4} yields an estimate of the thermodynamic-limit intercept c = F_l(\infty). This statement adopts the theoretical asymptotic exponent \gamma = -1/4 predicted for long-range-correlation-dominated finite-size errors.
      Refs. 13,14

Let $Z(r_{\mathrm{s}})$, $\gamma(r_{\mathrm{s}})$, and $\eta_1(r_{\mathrm{s}})$ be parameter functions obtained from one-shot GW calculations of the homogeneous electron gas as functions of the Wigner–Seitz density parameter $r_{\mathrm{s}}$; the transferability proposition is that for simple metallic elements whose valence electrons are approximately free-electron like (for example, sodium and aluminium) the HEG-derived parameter values evaluated at the material's $r_{\mathrm{s}}$ can be used without material-specific momentum-dependent corrections as input to the HEG-based effective-potential model, together with the plasmon energy set by the material average density $\bar\rho$, to produce qualitatively and approximately quantitatively correct total and momentum-resolved spectral functions of the crystalline metal within the model's level of approximation.

The proposition: Choosing closed-shell (“magic”) ground-state electron numbers and performing calculations at multiple finite system sizes provides sampling of shell-filling oscillatory finite-size effects adequate to permit an extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit when fitting to a model $F(N)=c+aN^{\gamma}$; specifically, the ground-state electron numbers used were for paramagnetic HEGs $N=26,50,74,114$ and for ferromagnetic HEGs $N=29,57,101$, and computing Fermi liquid parameters at this set of system sizes captures the dominant oscillatory behavior due to discrete reciprocal-lattice sampling that affects extrapolation.

The proposition: For Fermi liquid parameters $F_l$ (or $F_l/m^{*}$) of the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) computed in square periodic simulation cells containing $N$ electrons, the leading systematic finite-size correction arising from long-range correlation effects scales asymptotically as $N^{\gamma}$ with exponent $\gamma=-1/4$ (i.e., a correction term proportional to $N^{-1/4}$), so that finite-$N$ results can be extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting to the form $F_l(N)=c+aN^{-1/4}$; this scaling is the same as that argued in the theoretical analyses of long-range-correlation finite-size effects (Holzmann et al.).

The Fermi liquid parameters divided by the quasiparticle effective mass, $\;F_{l}^{s,a}/m^{*}$ (symmetric and antisymmetric channels for the paramagnetic HEG) and $F_{l}/m^{*}$ (ferromagnetic HEG), were obtained from the discrete-angle DMC interaction functions by first computing the Fourier components
      $$
      a_{l}^{\sigma\sigma'}=\frac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}N f_{\sigma\sigma'}(\theta)\cos(l\theta)\,d\theta,
      $$
      and then using the relations for an $N$-electron paramagnetic finite cell of density parameter $r_s$
      $$
      \frac{F_{l}^{s,a}}{m^{*}}=\frac{r_s^2}{4}\big[a_{l}^{\uparrow\uparrow}\pm a_{l}^{\uparrow\downarrow}\big],
      $$
      and for a ferromagnetic finite cell
      $$
      \frac{F_l}{m^{*}}=\frac{r_s^2}{4}a_{l}^{\uparrow\uparrow},
      $$
      where $m^{*}$ denotes the quasiparticle effective mass.  Numerical angular integration of the available nonuniform discrete angles $\{\theta_i\}$ was performed using a generalized composite Simpson's rule (piecewise quadratic interpolation adapted to a nonuniform grid), and the result was compared with a generalized composite trapezoidal rule to place an upper bound on the quadrature error.  Finite-size values of $F_l/m^{*}$ obtained at multiple closed-shell electron counts were extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit $N\to\infty$ by fitting the finite-$N$ data to the model $F_l(N)=c+aN^{\gamma}$ with the exponent fixed at $\gamma=-1/4$ (i.e., leading finite-size correction $\propto N^{-1/4}$) motivated by theoretical analyses of long-range-correlation contributions; ordinary (unweighted) least-squares fits were used because oscillatory shell-filling deviations of the finite-size data are larger than the nominal Monte Carlo error bars, and the quoted uncertainties on the extrapolated parameters reflect the scatter of the finite-$N$ data under these fits.
